storm.summary_layperson: Clear skies tonight with no storms anywhere near the area — the northwest breeze will carry through the evening without any weather concern.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Long Island Sound water at 70.5°F versus 79.6°F air creates a moderate stable marine layer, but with no storms inbound tonight it has nothing to moderate.
storm.detail_html:
The regional picture is clean: KOKX radar shows no storm cells in any approach sector as of 4:14 PM EDT, and no NWS watches, warnings, or advisories are active for the area. The NWS point forecast reads simply "Sunny" with a northwest wind — a clear, uneventful evening.

Instability is absent at the surface. HRRR places CAPE at 0 J/kg for this hour. The OKX Area Forecast Discussion does reference up to 1,500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE with 40-kt shear capable of severe thunderstorms — but that language concerns Tuesday–Wednesday's pattern, not tonight. The elevated 0–6 km shear (35.9 kt WSW) visible in the current data is simply an organizational parameter reflecting the northwest synoptic flow; without convective energy to fuel it, it carries no storm risk this evening.

The Long Island Sound marine stable layer is present at moderate strength: 70.5°F Sound water against 79.6°F air produces an 8.3°F temperature difference. This is just below the 10°F threshold where the pipeline classifies the protective mechanism as fully active, but a meaningful stable layer still sits over the water. With no convection approaching from any direction, the distinction is academic tonight.

What would change the call: an unexpected pop-up convective cell inland over Westchester or Fairfield County after 17:00 is the one scenario worth monitoring. The existing shear would organize such a cell quickly if it developed. With CAPE at 0 J/kg the probability is very low — but if you notice sharp cumulus build-up to the northwest after 5 PM, a quick radar check would confirm whether anything is forming.

wind.regime_label: Moderate gradient wind — no sea breeze

hourly.shift_note: steady NW gradient, best wind of the window
very light near sunset — borderline drifter

wind.tactics_blurb: The northwest gradient funnels to near-due-north at the cove by terrain — expect puffy, irregular conditions with no persistent favored side. Stay center course to average out the puffs; play each lift opportunistically rather than committing to a side.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Today is a moderate gradient-wind day — no sea breeze has established. The northwest synoptic flow has dominated all afternoon, fully suppressing any thermal sea-breeze circulation despite an 8.3°F air–water temperature difference (air 79.6°F vs Sound water 70.5°F) that would otherwise favor one. Every coastal station from the CT shore to the LI north shore is reporting northerly to northwesterly flow; the fraction of southerly coastal stations is 0.0% (0 of 20 stations). This is a gradient-only pattern with the authoritative airport mean at 9.4 kt — solidly in moderate-gradient territory.

At the cove, RYC WeatherLink reads due north (9°) at 10.6 kt with gusts to 18.9 kt — roughly 62° clockwise of HRRR's grid-point northwest (307°) and about 38° clockwise of the broader western-LIS ensemble median (NNW 331°). This offset is terrain channeling: northwest gradient flow crossing over land north of the cove arrives at a more northerly angle as it funnels between the shoreline features at the cove mouth. Expect on-water directions throughout the window to track closer to N–NNW than to the NW shown in the hourly table. The sea-breeze direction maturation correction does not apply on a gradient-wind day.

The wind story for 17:00–22:00 divides into two halves. From 17:00 to 18:00 the gradient delivers 7–8 kt mean with gusts to 12–14 kt — squarely in the one-design sweet spot (6–10 kt) and the best racing of the evening. Direction is steady NW with normal gust/lull cycling and terrain-driven puff irregularity. By 19:00 the mean is down to about 5 kt and approaching borderline drifter territory. Around sunset (~8:09 PM), HRRR bottoms at 3.5 kt NNW — below useful racing wind for most one-design classes. A modest nocturnal rebound to 4–6 kt is expected after 21:00 as light NW flow stabilizes, but this is unlikely to be race-quality. Unlike a collapsing sea breeze, there is no thermal trigger for this decline — the gradient itself is relaxing as daytime heating that reinforced the pressure pattern fades with the sun.

Course tactics: with north-to-northwest offshore flow in the cove, neither shore holds a persistent advantage. Per the cove geography, NW flow over land generates puffs and holes in irregular patterns; the center course averages the best pressure across the puff/lull cycle and avoids the deepest holes that form close to either shoreline. Oscillations of 15–30° are typical for this direction and source — play individual puffs opportunistically rather than committing to a lifted side. If you do see a shift persist across more than one puff sequence, it likely reflects a subtle gradient rotation rather than a sea-breeze oscillation — in that case consider committing to it.

Ensemble direction agreement is 0.61 — moderate coherence — consistent with an offshore turbulent regime where stations in different terrain positions report quite different directions. The APRS coefficient of variation is 0.86, confirming the spatial fragmentation of the wind field. This is a terrain-turbulence signature, not a boundary or front passage. No backdoor cold-front signal: KBOS is reporting WNW (not northeast), and we are well outside the April–mid-June backdoor seasonal window.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 (WLIS) wind reading 75 min stale — over-water speed may not reflect current conditions.
RAP CAPE unavailable; HRRR (0 J/kg) used as primary instability source — AFD CAPE citation (1,500 J/kg) applies to Tuesday–Wednesday, not tonight.
KFOK anemometer down — no wind data from Westhampton Beach LI (station excluded from ensemble).
